text:
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Accident Analysis and Prevention
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/aap
Eﬀects of mobile phone distraction on pedestrians’ crossing behavior and
visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection: An outdoor
experimental study
Kang Jianga, Feiyang Linga, Zhongxiang Fenga,⁎, Changxi Mab, Wesley Kumferc, Chen Shaoa,
Kun Wanga
a School of Automobile and Traﬃc Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, Anhui Province, China
b School of Traﬃc and Transportation, Lanzhou Jiaotong University, Lanzhou 730070, China
c Highway Safety Research Center, University of North Carolina, 730 Martin Luther King Jr Blvd, Suite 300, Chapel Hill, NC, 27599-3430, United States
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Pedestrian
Crossing safety
Mobile phone
Distraction
Visual attention
Eye movement
A B S T R A C T
With the rapid growth in mobile phone use worldwide, traﬃc safety experts have begun to consider the impact
of mobile phone distractions on pedestrian crossing safety. This study sought to investigate how mobile phone
distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction and text distraction) aﬀect the behavior of pe-
destri

ans while they are crossing the street. An outdoor-environment experiment was conducted among 28
college student pedestrians. Two HD videos and an eye tracker were employed to record and analyze crossing
behavior and visual attention allocation. The results of the research showed that the three mobile phone dis-
tractions cause diﬀerent levels of impairment to pedestrians’ crossing performance, with the greatest eﬀect from
text distraction, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction. Pedestrians distracted by
music initiate crossing later, have increased pupil diameter, and reduce their scanning frequency, ﬁxation points
and ﬁxation times toward traﬃc signal area priorities. In addition to the above eﬀects, pedestrians distracted by
phone conversation cross the street more slowly, direct fewer ﬁxation points to the right traﬃc area, and spend
less ﬁxation time and lower average ﬁxation duration on the left traﬃc area. Moreover, pedestrians distracted by
texting look left and right less often and switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traﬃc
environment. These ﬁndings may inform researchers, policy makers, and pedestrians.
1. Introduction
A

s the prevalence of mobile phones has increased, the number of
pedestrians who use mobile phones while crossing the street has risen
globally. According to a recent observational study in China, approxi-
mately one-third of pedestrians displayed mobile-phone-distracted ac-
tivity while crossing the street (Zhao et al., 2015). An authoritative poll
in the United States even indicated that approximately 60% of adults
admitted using mobile phones while crossing the street (Liberty Mutual
Insurance, 2013).
Pedestrians’ mobile phone use behavior while crossing, which is a
typical multi-tasking activity like distracted driving, requires them to
complete two complex tasks simultaneously. First, pedestrians must
observe the surrounding traﬃc environment carefully, including ap-
proaching vehicles, traﬃc signs, and signals, and must assess vehicles’
traveling speed, distance and arrival time to ensure their crossing
safety. Second, pedestrians are compelled to pay visual, auditory and
cognitive attention to check and operate their mobile phones to keep in
touch with their social circle. Thus, mobile phone use distracts pedes-
trians’ attention from the complex traﬃc environment, which may
c

ause pedestrians to miss critical traﬃc information and to make in-
correct assessments and be exposed to a higher risk of collision
(Bungum et al., 2005; Tabibi and Pfeﬀer, 2007). However, mobile
phone use behavior while crossing is not always considered an im-
minent threat by most pedestrians. This behavior has received less so-
cial attention and fewer legal restraints than mobile phone use behavior
while driving.
Despite being often overlooked, mobile phone distraction has be-
come recognized in the academic ﬁeld as one of the major contributors
to fatal and non-fatal pedestrian crashes. Injury data from the US
Consumer Product Safety Commission indicates that the number of
mobile-phone-related injuries among pedestrians increased during the
period from 2005 to 2010, and in 2010, it even exceeded the number of
injuries for drivers (Nasar and Troyer, 2013).
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aap.2018.03.019

Accident Analysis and Prevent

ion 115 (2018) 170–177
Available online 28 March 2018
0001-4575/ © 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
T

Meanwhile, the negative eﬀects of mobile phone distraction on
pedestrians’ crossing performance have been examined by numerous
studies, including observational surveys and virtual-environment ex-
periments. Mobile phone distraction was reported to have a signiﬁcant
eﬀect on pedestrians’ gait quality according to a gait analysis device
(Mcdonough et al., 2008; Lamberg and Muratori, 2012; Kim et al.,
2014; Sammy et al., 2015; Nakamura et al., 2016). Participants engaged
in cell phone use presented signiﬁcant reductions in gait velocity and
cadence as well as step and stride lengths; they also showed a tendency
to increase step width and step time. These changed gait characteristics
would have further impacts on pedestrians’ walking stability and
variability. Furthermore, mobile phone distraction impairs pedestrians’
crossing behavior and increases their vulnerability to conﬂicting traﬃc
streams. For example, pedestrians distracted by mobile phones walk
more slowly (Hatﬁeld and Murphy, 2007; Hyman et al., 2010; Schabrun
et al., 2014), change directions more often (Hyman et al., 2010), take
longer to initiate crossing (Stavrinos et al., 2009; Neider et al., 2010,
2011; Byington and Sch

webel, 2013), miss more safe opportunities to
cross (Neider et al., 2010; Stavrinos et al., 2011; Byington and
Schwebel, 2013), are more likely to cross unsafely into oncoming traﬃc
(Nasar et al., 2008; Stavrinos et al., 2009, 2011; Schwebel et al., 2012;
Byington and Schwebel, 2013; Dalibor et al., 2016), and make more
errors (Bellows et al., 2012) than pedestrians who are not distracted.
Additionally, mobile phone distraction draws pedestrians’ attention
away from the crossing task and aﬀects their visual attention distribu-
tion. Numerous studies have examined how mobile phone distraction
impairs pedestrians’ visual attention through the observation of parti-
cipants’ head movements. For example, pedestrians distracted by mo-
bile phones are less likely to look at traﬃc before crossing (Hatﬁeld and
Murphy, 2007; Stavrinos et al., 2009, 2011; Byington and Schwebel,
2013; Dalibor et al., 2016), look left and right less while crossing
(Hatﬁeld and Murphy, 2007; Dalibor et al., 2016), spend more time
looking away from the road (Schwebel et al., 2012; Byington and
Schwebel, 2013) and show a decreased perceptual visual ﬁeld (Alejalil
and Davoodi, 2017) compared to those who are not di

stracted. With the
advancement of science and technology, eye-tracking systems can be
successfully applied to explore pedestrians’ visual search strategies in
road crossing tasks (Tapiro et al., 2014, 2015; Tapiro et al., 2016a; Lin
and Huang, 2017; Lu and Lo, 2017). Tapiro et al. (2016b) also con-
ducted a semi-immersive virtual environment simulation to assess how
mobile phone conversations aﬀect visual attention distribution. The
data derived from recordings of pedestrians’ eye-movements have
shown that participants distracted by mobile phone conversations
spend relatively more time looking at the center area and less time
looking at the periphery where unseen threats may emerge. In brief,
these unsafe crossing behaviors and the impaired visual attention
caused by mobile phone distraction may increase pedestrian vulner-
ability and risk of accident.
However, the distraction level of previous observational experi-
ments was uncontrolled, and the degree of perceived risk in virtual-
environment experiments may be diﬀerent from outdoor-environment
experiments. Furthermore, pedestrians’ visual search performance
under other conditions of distraction, such as when listening to music

or
when texting, has not been examined or compared in previous studies
(Tapiro et al., 2016b). Additionally, the total ﬁxation time was used as
the only eye movement indicator to describe pedestrians’ visual atten-
tion distribution, so other eye movement indicators, such as ﬁxation
points and ﬁxation duration, should be considered to enhance the
measurement of and better understand pedestrian distraction.
Therefore, the present study conducted an outdoor-environment
experiment to explore the eﬀect of mobile phone distraction among
college student pedestrians. The size of the eﬀects between diﬀerent
uses of mobile phones while crossing (listening to music, talking on the
phone, and texting) were compared. As dependent variables, three
crossing behavior indictors and ﬁve eye movement indicators were
recorded together to quantify the eﬀects of mobile phone distraction on
pedestrians’ crossing performance.
2. Method
2.1. Participants
Interested participants were recruited from Hefei University of
Technology in China. A total of twenty-eight college students, 17 male
and 11 female, participated in the study. The twenty-eight participants
ranged in age from 17 to 25 years old with a mea

n age of 20.6 years
(SD = 2.23), and all of them owned smart phones. This group was
chosen since college students are known to cross streets more fre-
quently and to use mobile phones with greater frequency than other
groups. According to the preliminary questionnaire survey (Jiang et al.,
2017), approximately 40% of 405 college students reported having
used mobile phones while crossing, and 5.4% of the respondents had
even been involved in a crossing accident due to mobile phone dis-
tractions. The principal inclusion criteria in the study were that parti-
cipants did not wear glasses (so that eye movement could be properly
tracked) and had the ability to walk unimpaired and at a standard pace.
After ﬁnishing an approximately 30-minute experiment, the partici-
pants received nearly $8 as compensation for their eﬀort.
2.2. Experiment scenario
To better simulate the eﬀect of mobile phone distraction on pe-
destrians’ crossing behavior and visual attention allocation, an outdoor-
environment experiment was conducted. The experiment was con-
ducted from December 28, 2016, to January 10, 2017, during sunny
weather. Considering the greater possibility that pedestrians would use
their mo

bile phones at such an intersection in everyday life and for
greater security during our experiment, a two-phase traﬃc-signal-con-
trolled intersection with a 40-km/h speed limited was selected as the
experimental intersection. The intersection was located near a re-
sidential area with rater complete necessary street furniture and was
relatively quiet and separated from busy urban areas. Because the ex-
periment time (9:00–11:00 a.m. and 2:00–4:00 p.m.) avoided peak hour
and weekends, the experimental intersection had a low level of traﬃc,
particularly in the right lane, and the number of vehicles per minute
was fewer than 5 based on our observation. The 14-meter-wide ex-
periment crosswalk with zebra crossing provided twenty-one seconds of
green light time during which pedestrians were given a crossing signal;
this green interval alternated with a 39-s red phase during which pe-
destrians were forbidden from crossing. As shown in Fig. 1, for each
direction, there was a lane designed for both through traﬃc and left-
turn traﬃc and a lane designed for both through traﬃc and right-turn
traﬃc.
2.3. Apparatus
In the current study, pedestrians’ crossing behaviors were captured
and reco

rded by two Sony video HD cameras mounted on tripods. The
cameras were placed in secret hiding places facing pedestrians to ensure
they would not draw attention and distract the crossing pedestrians,
thereby confounding the study results. Because eye movement re-
cording has been considered an appropriate research tool to identify
drivers’ visual attention in terms of distracted driving research
(Garrison, 2011; Garrison and Williams, 2013), Tobii Pro Glasses 2
were used to record the pedestrians’ eye movements. 50-HZ Tobii Pro
Glasses 2 with a wearable eye-tracker apparatus can capture objective
data and allow insights into human behavior in any real-world en-
vironment by measuring a variety of eye movement and attentional
behaviors. They can also record and store eye-tracking data, sounds,
and footage on a removable SD memory card for further analysis.
Considering the inﬂuence of diﬀerent mobile phones, the Huawei P9
with an Android system and a 5.2 inch screen was selected as the only
K. Jiang et al.
Accident Analysis and Prevention 115 (2018) 170–177
171

experimental mobile phone.
2.4. Experimental design
Prior to the experiment, each participant was instructed regarding
the requirements of the experiment. The participants read and signed a
written informed consent document explaining the instructions, pro-
cedures and potential risks of the study. Participants were informed that
they should maintain their normal walking speed they would use to
cross the street and should endeavor to complete the mobile phone task
with the promise of guaranteed security. In addition, the most im-
portant premise of the experiment was that all the participants should
cross the street within the zebra crossing and obey the traﬃc signal.
To eliminate the impact of the unfamiliar experimental phone,
participants were familiarized with the phone before the experiment by
practicing the three types of mobile phone operations until they were
skilled at them. Only after conﬁrming that the participants had no
problem operating the experimental phone was the experiment allowed
to proceed to the next stage. Every participant was required to wear and
calibrate the Tobii Pro Glasses 2 with the help of the experimenter.
Once the eye tracker calibration was comple

ted, each participant
started familiarization session that lasted 10–20 min to eliminate any
discomfort. During the familiarization session, participants were asked
to take a walk, look far away and complete a brief demographic ques-
tionnaire that yielded self-reported information. Fortunately, no parti-
cipants withdrew from the experiment due to discomfort or for any
other reason.
The participants entered the outdoor road-crossing environment
that included 4 pedestrian crossings around the intersection. They were
asked to walk along the intersection counter-clockwise and initiate
another lap with a 2-minute rest after returning to the origin. When
completing a pedestrian crossing, participants were instructed to wait
for a complete red light and then start the next pedestrian crossing after
the green light became lit. During this period, participants stood on
each corner for nearly 60 s and were asked to put the phone into their
pocket and take a rest. Another experimenter was assigned to follow the
participant without interference to guarantee the participant’s safety.
The experimenter was required to look left and right frequently to
identify potential dangers, particularly pa

ying attention to the speed
and distance of oncoming motor vehicles and non-motor vehicles. Only
if the participants were in a relatively safe state would the experiment
continue. In case of emergencies, the experimenter was asked to remain
half a step away from the participants so that they could stop the
participant’s dangerous behavior in time. In total, each participant was
asked to undertake 8 crossing trials, ﬁve undistracted with four not
recorded and analyzed and three while distracted by the phone con-
versation, text and music conditions presented in ﬁxed order (outlined
in Fig. 1).
Undistracted (S0): Participants did not use a mobile phone or any
other distracting device while crossing the street. After waiting for a
complete red light, participants could make a decision to start crossing
once the green light became lit. Participants ﬁnished the crossing task
by observing traﬃc regulations and then waited for the next crossing
trial. During this period, participants did not receive any instructions
from the experimenter.
Music distraction (S1): Participants completed the crossing trial
while listening to music. The experimenter sent a message to require
participants to w

ear the headphones and listen to the music with only
the climax while waiting during the 39-second countdown of the red
light. As a caveat, the time experimenter gave instructions through the
mobile phone determined based on numerous pre-experiments to en-
sure that the participants were immersed in the mobile phone distrac-
tion tasks during the 10-second countdown of the red light. The re-
maining 29 s were used to prepare the participants to wear the
headphones. During the approximately 10-second period of countdown
of the red light, the participants opened the phone and then selected
and played the music. The participants could make a decision to start
crossing once the green light became lit. During the crossing trial,
participants were immersed in the music. After ﬁnishing the trial,
participants were required to proceed to the next trial and put away the
phone.
Phone conversation distraction (S2): Participants completed the
crossing while engaging in mental arithmetic tasks by phone con-
servation. Participants were asked to answer three arithmetic multi-
plication questions below ten carefully as there was no chance to revise
their answers. The experimenter began dialing th

e call during the 25-s
countdown of the red light. With nearly 10 s left in the countdown of
the red light, participants were preparing to operate and answer the
phone. The participants could make a decision to start crossing once the
light became green. The mental-arithmetic conversation was con-
tinuously implemented until the crossing trial was complete. Then, the
participants proceeded to the next trial and put away the phone.
Text distraction (S3): Participants completed the crossing while
engaging in mental arithmetic tasks by text message. Participants were
Fig. 1. Experimental scenario.
K. Jiang et al.
Accident Analysis and Prevention 115 (2018) 170–177
172

instructed to open the message previously sent by the experimenter and
then reply to three arithmetic multiplication tasks below ten in ac-
cordance with the requirements. The experimenter sent the message
with 20 s remaining in the countdown of the red light. With nearly 10 s
left in the countdown of the red light, participants were preparing to
open the phone and check the text message. The participants could
make a decision to start crossing once the light turned green. During the
crossing, participants were asked to text and respond to the mental-
arithmetic conversation. After ﬁnishing the trial, participants pro-
ceeded to the next trial and put away the phone.
2.5. Measures
2.5.1. Crossing behavior
A frame-by-frame analysis of video recordings captured by two HD
video cameras was conducted to evaluate the pedestrians’ crossing
behavior. The following three indicators were computed: (1) initiate
time - the amount of time between the green light becoming lit and the
participant initiating crossing; (2) look left and right - a measure of
attention to traﬃc based on the number of times participants looked left
and right while crossing the street; (3) crossing speed - the mean
wa

lking speed between the pedestrian initiating crossing and ﬁnishing
crossing.
2.5.2. Visual attention
The eye-tracking data and videos made with the Tobii Pro Glasses 2
were processed, analyzed and exported by a dedicated software tool
called the Tobii Pro Glasses Analyzer. Some eye movement indexes,
including pupil diameters and scanning numbers, could be output di-
rectly through the Tobii Pro Glasses Analyzer. However, secondary
processing required analysis of the camera videos with ﬁxation points
captured by the Tobii Pro Glasses 2 to determine where the pedestrians
were going to ﬁxate accurately. Eye movement videos of two pedes-
trians were selected randomly, and their visual ﬁxation targets were
recorded and classiﬁed from ﬁxation to ﬁxation. Then, the pedestrians’
visual ﬁxation sources were divided into ﬁve main areas of interest
(AOIs) comprising the mobile phone area, crosswalk area, traﬃc signal
area, right traﬃc area and left traﬃc area. Based on these AOI deﬁni-
tions, the ﬁxation points, ﬁxation time and mean ﬁxation duration on
each AOI could be calculated. The following ﬁve dependent variables
were deﬁned in this study to identify pedestrians’ attention allocation

patterns objectively: (1) pupil diameter - the mean pupil diameter of the
left eye was selected to reﬂect the eﬀort of the individual’s information
processing; (2) scanning frequency – the scanning numbers per unit
time were recorded to weigh the individual’s visual alertness to the
surrounding traﬃc environment; (3) ﬁxation points – the ﬁxation
points were counted to reveal how the individual’s visual attention
switched between the ﬁve AOIs; (4) ﬁxation time – the ﬁxation time
was chosen to measure how the individual’s limited visual attention
resources were distributed on each AOI; (5) ﬁxation duration – the
mean ﬁxation duration was used to describe how the individual’s visual
attention was maintained on each AOI.
3. Results
The eight dependent variables were continuously recorded for all
participants across the four distraction conditions (i.e., undistracted,
music distraction, phone conversation distraction and text distraction).
To determine the eﬀects of mobile phone distractions on pedestrians’
crossing performance, all measures were submitted to a repeated-
measure analysis of variance (ANOVA) in SPSS 19.0. Then, post hoc
comparisons were performed to evaluate diﬀerences b

etween the four
distraction conditions in cases where the omnibus ANOVA was sig-
niﬁcant. To control for family-wise error, all post hoc comparisons were
evaluated using a conservative Bonferroni-corrected alpha.
4. Crossing behavior
Pedestrians took 0.69 s (SD = 0.61) to initiate crossing for the un-
distracted condition, whereas for the music distraction, phone con-
versation distraction and text distraction, pedestrians needed 1.28 s
(SD = 1.02), 1.51 s (SD = 1.00), and 2.65 s (SD = 2.34), respectively.
With regard to traﬃc attention, pedestrians looked left and right while
crossing the street 4.46 times (SD = 2.93), 4.32 times (SD = 2.34), 4.29
times (SD = 3.45) and 2.50 times (SD = 1.50) for the above four con-
ditions, respectively. Simultaneously, the participants walked an
average of 1.46 m/s (SD = 0.19) for the undistracted condition,
whereas for music distraction, phone conversation distraction and text
distraction, participants walked an average of 1.45 m/s (SD = 0.17),
1.37 m/s (SD = 0.17) and 1.36 m/s (SD = 0.16), respectively.
Signiﬁcant diﬀerences between conditions were found in all three
crossing
behavior
variables
(Initiate
time:
F(3,108) = 10.09,
p < 0.001, parti

al η2 = 0.30; Look left and right: F(3,108) = 7.54,
p < 0.001,
partial
η2 = 0.21;
Crossing
speed:
F(3,108) = 3.78,
p = 0.01, partial η2 = 0.14). Then, post-hoc analyses were conducted to
further examine the eﬀect of distraction across the various conditions.
Further investigation (Table 1) suggested that participants spent more
time initiating crossing when distracted by music (p = 0.012), phone
conversation (p < 0.001) and text (p < 0.001) compared to when
they were undistracted. Participants distracted by text also spent more
time initiating crossing than when distracted by music (p = 0.009) and
phone conversation (p = 0.025). As shown in Table 1, participants
looked left and right fewer times when engaging in a text distraction
than when engaging in a phone conversation distraction (p = 0.005),
when engaging in a music distraction (p < 0.001) or when un-
distracted (p = 0.001). Moreover, participants distracted by phone
conversation (p = 0.015) and text (p = 0.032) crossed the street at a
slower speed than when undistracted. The crossing speed under music
distraction and text distraction also showed signiﬁcant diﬀerences
(p = 0.017).

5. Visual attention
The mean pupil diameter was 2.75 mm (SD = 0.35) under the un-
distracted condition, 2.86 mm (SD = 0.39) under music distraction,
2.86 mm (SD = 0.36) under phone conversation distraction, and
2.88 mm (SD = 0.38) under text distraction. The diﬀerence between
conditions
was
signiﬁcant
(F(3,108) = 10.90,
p < 0.001,
partial
η2 = 0.41), and the participants under the undistracted condition
showed
smaller
pupil
diameter
than
under
music
distraction
(p = 0.002), phone conversation distraction (p < 0.001) and text dis-
traction (p < 0.001). For the scanning frequency, the ANOVA also
indicated signiﬁcant diﬀerences between conditions (F(3,108) = 12.21,
p < 0.001, partial η2 = 0.43). The scanning frequency for music dis-
traction (mean = 6.00, SD = 1.05, p < 0.001), phone conversation
distraction (mean = 5.72, SD = 1.68, p = 0.012) and text distraction
(mean = 4.91, SD = 1.16, p < 0.001) was signiﬁcantly lower than for
the undistracted condition (mean = 6.82, SD = 1.13). The scanning
frequency for music distraction (p = 0.002) and phone conversation
distraction (p = 0.019) also had signiﬁcant diﬀerences compared with
text distraction.
The number of ﬁxation points toward di

ﬀerent AOIs under the four
experimental conditions is presented in Table 2. There were signiﬁcant
diﬀerences in the number of ﬁxation points toward diﬀerent AOIs be-
tween conditions (Phone areas: F(3,108) = 10.09, p < 0.001, partial
η2 = 0.84;
Crosswalk
area:
F(3,108) = 38.01,
p < 0.001,
partial
η2 = 0.70; Traﬃc signal area: F(3,108) = 6.86, p = 0.001, partial
η2 = 0.30; Right traﬃc: F(3,108) = 4.43, p = 0.008, partial η2 = 0.22;
Left traﬃc: F(3,108) = 7.81, p = 0.008, partial η2 = 0.33). Post-hoc
analyses demonstrate that the number of ﬁxation points toward the
phone area for the undistracted condition was signiﬁcantly less than for
the music distraction (p = 0.002), phone conversation distraction
(p = 0.001) and text distraction (p < 0.001). Moreover, the numbers
of ﬁxation points for music distraction (p < 0.001) and phone con-
versation distraction (p < 0.001) were less than for text distraction.
With regard to the crosswalk area, the number of ﬁxation points for text
distraction was signiﬁcantly less than for the undistracted condition
(p < 0.001), music distraction (p < 0.001) and phone conversation
distraction (p < 0.001). Regarding the traﬃc signal area, the numbers
of ﬁxa

tion points for music distraction (p = 0.007), phone conversation
(p = 0.026) and text distraction (p = 0.002) were signiﬁcantly less than
for the undistracted condition. The number of ﬁxation points toward
the traﬃc signal area under music distraction and text distraction also
showed a signiﬁcant diﬀerence (p = 0.017). Regarding the right traﬃc
area, the number of ﬁxation points for phone conversation distraction
(p = 0.043) and text distraction (p = 0.005) was signiﬁcantly less than
for the undistracted condition. In addition, Fig. 2 shows a signiﬁcant
diﬀerence between music distraction and text distraction in the number
of ﬁxation points (p = 0.014). In terms of the left traﬃc area, the
number of ﬁxation points for text distraction was signiﬁcantly less than
for

condition
(p < 0.001),

distraction
(p = 0.003) and phone conversation distraction (p = 0.012).

Left traﬃc: F(3,108) = 6.72, p = 0.008, partial η2 = 0.30). Post-hoc
analyses show that the total ﬁxation time on the phone area under the
undistracted

was

than

music
distraction
(p = 0.002), phone conversation distraction (p < 0.001) and text dis-

traction (p < 0.001) and phone conversation distraction (p < 0.001)
were also less than that for text distraction. Regarding the crosswalk
area, the total ﬁxation time under text distraction was signiﬁcantly less
than under the undistracted condition (p < 0.001), music distraction
(p < 0.001) and phone conversation distraction (p < 0.001). In ad-
dition, Fig. 3 shows a signiﬁcant diﬀerence between the undistracted
condition and music distraction on the total ﬁxation time (p = 0.012).
Regarding the traﬃc signal area, the total ﬁxation time under the four
experimental conditions showed signiﬁcant diﬀerences except for music
distraction and phone conversation distraction (p = 0.787). In terms of
th

e right traﬃc area, the total ﬁxation time under text distraction was
signiﬁcantly less than for the undistracted condition (p = 0.004) and
music distraction (p = 0.020). For the left traﬃc area, only the un-
distracted condition and the music condition had no signiﬁcant diﬀer-
ence in the total ﬁxation time (p = 0.545).

distraction (p < 0.001). For the crosswalk area, the mean ﬁxation

6. Discussion
The present study focuses on pedestrian crossing performance while
operating a mobile phone in an outdoor road environment. This study
also examines the diﬀerences in college students’ pedestrian behavior
while distracted by music, phone conversation and text. Three crossing
behavior variables recorded by HD video and ﬁve eye movement
variables captured by Tobii Pro Glasses 2 were chosen to m

easure the
eﬀect of mobile phone distraction on pedestrian crossing performance.
In short, the results suggest that music, phone conversation and text all
distract college pedestrians in their level of crossing performance, with
text distraction associated with the highest impairment, followed by
phone conversation distraction and music distraction.
Music distraction had the least inﬂuence on pedestrian crossing
performance, which is similar to the ﬁndings of previous works on safe
automobile driving (Bellinger et al., 2009; Dibben and Williamson,
2007) and an observational study on pedestrian crossing behavior
(Walker et al., 2012). This result is likely because listening to music
involves lessdue to the cognitive complexity and visual distraction of
listening to music than phone conversation or text operation. However,
the increased pupil diameter indicated that music distraction does
somewhat generate cognitive load. There is an increased cognitive load
from a distracting device that reduces the eﬃcacy of the cognitive
process of perceiving and processing relevant environmental stimuli
and then making a delayed decision to initiate motoric processes when
the traﬃc signal becomes

green. When individuals are immersed in
music distraction, they may have no extra cognitive attention to con-
centrate on the surrounding traﬃc environment. Thus, they have to
reduce their scanning frequency and their ﬁxation points and ﬁxation
times toward traﬃc signal area priority. However, compared to pe-
destrians who are undistracted, pedestrians who are distracted by music
invest slightly more (rather than fewer) ﬁxation points and ﬁxation
time toward the left traﬃc area, although no signiﬁcant diﬀerence was
found. Unlike the other distractions, listening to music creates a

K. Jiang et al.
Accident Analysis and Prevention 115 (2018) 170–177
175

constant disruption of auditory signals, which impairs aural cues from a
distance. Thus, to compensate for the auditory loss, individuals may try
to allocate more visual attention to the left traﬃc area to judge the
safety of the environment.
Regarding the phone conversation distraction, pedestrians also
crossed the street at a lower average speed. Similar ﬁndings have been
found in studies investigating the impact of phone conversation
(Hyman et al., 2010) and texting (Lopresti-Goodman et al., 2012) while
walking. One possible explanation for the lower crossing speed is that
individuals who are distracted by phone conversations attempt to be-
have more cautiously than those who are not distracted. However, the
slower crossing speed could lead to pedestrians failing to ﬁnish crossing
during the eﬀective green light time. In addition to the traﬃc signal
area and crosswalk area, pedestrians distracted by phone conversations
focus less on ﬁxation points of the right traﬃc area and spend less
ﬁxation time and average ﬁxation duration on the left traﬃc area
compared with the undistracted condition. On the one hand, fewer
ﬁxation points would make it easy to miss important traﬃc informat

ion
and emergencies; on the other hand, less ﬁxation duration may result in
incorrect judgments due to careless observation. All of these unsafe
behaviors can put pedestrians in danger. However, it is interesting that
the look left and right variable extracted from the video shows no
signiﬁcant diﬀerences between phone conversation distraction and the
undistracted condition. From this ﬁnding, we can see that eye move-
ment data captured by the eye tracker is an eﬀective complement to the
video data, which could determine individuals’ visual attention allo-
cation more accurately.
For text distraction, all three crossing behavior variables and ﬁve
visual attention variables were signiﬁcantly diﬀerent from the un-
distracted condition. Texting messages while crossing the street, which
involves both communication interchanges and reading and typing,
may be more cognitively distracting and demanding than phone con-
versation distraction and music distraction. Pedestrians distracted by
text experience more diﬃculty navigating a complex environment than
do people under other conditions. As expected, pedestrians distracted
by text initiated crossing later, looked left and right less and w

alked
slower. Field studies (e.g., Lopresti-Goodman et al., 2012; Schwebel
et al., 2012; Perlmutter et al., 2014) have provided partial conﬁrmation
of these ﬁndings. Texting, which involves not only cognitive distraction
but also visual distraction, presents a major threat to pedestrians’ visual
attention allocation. Text operation occupies most of the limited visual
attention so that individuals scan with lower frequency, focus less on
ﬁxation points, and spend less ﬁxation time and average ﬁxation
duration on the crosswalk area, traﬃc signal area, right traﬃc area and
left traﬃc area.
Overall, the crossing behavior variables and visual attention vari-
ables in the current study verify that the three types of mobile phone
distractions studied are harmful to pedestrian crossing performance.
Given the prominence of mobile phone distractions that are now in-
herent in everyday living, our ﬁndings highlight the need for continued
experimental research on the eﬀects of these distractions, particularly
within the larger context of mixed traﬃc within the intersection area.
While eﬀorts should certainly be made to outreach to pedestrians to
combat distracted walking (and one intervention

study from Schwebel
et al. (2017) in 2017 shows some limited beneﬁt to self-reported in-
tentions and thoughts), these eﬀorts should not be conducted without
also considering design elements and driver regulations. Improving
pedestrian safety will likely require both discouraging risky pedestrian
behavior and encouraging sound designs, such as limiting turning
movements, to avoid creating those hazards distracted pedestrians may
miss. The ﬁndings also have some implications for pedestrian safety
policy. Although most people in China would not accept laws prohi-
biting distracted crossing at this stage, this measure has apparently
decreased the crash and injury rates for distracted driving (Huang et al.,
2010; Jacobson and Gostin, 2010). Implementing and enforcing pro-
hibitions on distracted crossing behavior will be very challenging but
might be eﬀective if used in conjunction with other measures to im-
prove pedestrian safety. Intervention eﬀorts are not always eﬀective if
used as the sole countermeasure, but this study highlights one aspect of
the complex problem of safety at intersections. Recognizing pedestrian
behavior may inform design policies and driver regulations to aﬀor

d
more protection for pedestrians who may be distracted and are more
vulnerable to conﬂicts with other transportation modes.
There are also some limitations in this study. First, the small sample
size and the limiting of the sample to college students may limit the
degree to which the ﬁndings of this study can be generalized to the
overall pedestrian population. Future research could extend the work to
a more varied sample. Second, participants were restricted from self-
regulation and behavioral adaptation in this experiment, so we do not
know whether participants would use mobile phones in the same way
during daily life. Third, the data collection was limited to one speciﬁc
signalized intersection with the very low traﬃc volume. We strongly
recommend testing this approach in other environments, such as non-
signalized intersections or intersections with high traﬃc volume, to
investigate how mobile phone distraction aﬀects pedestrian crossing
safety depending on the complexity of the road situation. Fourth, the
not exactly conformity between the experimental design and partici-
pants’ daily life may also be one of the limitations, including the use of
unfamiliar phone and the sele

ction of mental arithmetic for the con-
versation and texting. Finally, it should be noted that other mobile
phone tasks, such as mobile internet, could be studied for a fuller un-
derstanding of mobile phone distraction and pedestrian crossing safety.
7. Conclusion
In the present study, three crossing behavior variables and ﬁve vi-
sual attention variables were shown to eﬀectively assess the eﬀects of
mobile
phone
distraction
on
pedestrians’
crossing
performance.
Pedestrians distracted by music, phone conversation and text initiate
crossing later, increase pupil diameter, and reduce scanning frequency
as well as ﬁxation points and ﬁxation times toward traﬃc signal area
priority. Pedestrians distracted by phone conversation cross the street
more slowly, have fewer ﬁxation points to the right traﬃc area, and
spend less ﬁxation time and average ﬁxation duration on the left traﬃc
area. When distracted by text, pedestrians also look left and right less
often and switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the
traﬃc environment. Furthermore, the results show that text distraction
interferes the most with pedestrian crossing performance, particularly
visual attention allocat

ion strategies. Phone conversation distraction
and music distraction also have a certain impact on pedestrian crossing
performance. These ﬁndings may provide useful information for re-
searchers, policy makers, and pedestrians.
